title: Extending mixtime
text: Time units building blocks calendars. Time units S7 classes created new_time_unit(), inherit mt_unit (purely abstract time unit class). practice, time units inherit either: mt_tz_unit civil-time units whose boundaries depend timezone (e.g. months, days, hours, …), mt_loc_unit astronomical-time units whose boundaries depend geographic location (e.g. solar days). time units accept numeric n argument representing size unit (e.g. 1L “one month”). Additional arguments needed time unit can defined S7 : inheriting properties mt_tz_unit (tz IANA names) mt_loc_unit (lon, lat, alt), defining custom S7 properties new_time_unit(properties = ...). name time unit’s S7 class becomes internal identifier unit, unique across calendars. class name important users calendar, descriptive developers. time units typically exported within calendar object (shown ) accessed via $ notation.
code: # Timezone-aware Symmetry454 year and month units new_time_unit("tu_symmetry454_year",  parent = mt_tz_unit) #> <tu_symmetry454_year> class #> @ parent     : <mixtime::mt_tz_unit> #> @ constructor: function(...) {...} #> @ validator  : <NULL> #> @ properties : #>  $ n : <integer> or <double> #>  $ tz: <character> new_time_unit("tu_symmetry454_month", parent = mt_tz_unit) #> <tu_symmetry454_month> class #> @ parent     : <mixtime::mt_tz_unit> #> @ constructor: function(...) {...} #> @ validator  : <NULL> #> @ properties : #>  $ n : <integer> or <double> #>  $ tz: <character>

title: Extending mixtime
text: cardinality number finer units fit inside coarser unit. example, 7 days week, cardinality cal_isoweek$day(1L) terms cal_isoweek$week(1L) 7. units fixed relationship (e.g. 7 days per week) argument can ignored. variable disambiguates point time variable cardinalities (e.g. days per month), internal numeric representation time coarser unit (e.g. months since epoch days → months). Symmetry454 calendar every year exactly 12 months (fixed relationship). number weeks month follows repeating 4–5–4 pattern across quarter (months 1–3: 4, 5, 4 weeks; months 4–6: 4, 5, 4 weeks; ). circsum() helper computes many weeks fall n-month period summing appropriate slice cycle, especially useful month unit size greater 1. Symmetry454 uses 293-year leap-week cycle: year leap year (gaining 53rd week December) (52 * year + 146) %% 293 < 52. differs substantially familiar Gregorian leap-day rule produces 52 leap years every 293-year period. Cardinality methods required adjacent time units (e.g. month → year, week → month), can defined non-adjacent units optimization. direct cardinality method defined, mixtime attempt derive computing cardinality along path intermediate units. cardinality methods also define units finer/coarser operations requiring graph traversal, essential first unit finer unit second unit coarser unit. inverse cardinality method (units flipped) automatically derived, define one direction (finer → coarser) relationship.
code: chronon_cardinality(cal_isoweek$day(1L), cal_isoweek$week(1L)) #> [1] 7 chronon_cardinality(cal_gregorian$day(1L), cal_gregorian$month(1L), at = 0L) # Jan 1970 #> [1] 31 chronon_cardinality(cal_gregorian$day(1L), cal_gregorian$month(1L), at = 1L) # Feb 1970 #> [1] 28 chronon_cardinality(cal_gregorian$day(1L), cal_gregorian$month(1L), at = 25L) # Feb 1972 (leap year) #> [1] 29 # Each Symmetry454 year has 12 months S7::method(chronon_cardinality, list(cal_symmetry454$month, cal_symmetry454$year)) <-   function(x, y, at = NULL) {     y@n * 12L / x@n   } chronon_cardinality(cal_symmetry454$month(1L), cal_symmetry454$year(1L)) #> [1] 12 S7::method(chronon_cardinality, list(cal_symmetry454$week, cal_symmetry454$month)) <-   function(x, y, at = NULL) {     # The number of weeks in each n-month period     month_size <- y@n     nweeks_cyc <- circsum(c(4L, 5L, 4L), month_size)      # Find which n-month period we're in based on the "at" position (months since epoch)     period <- at %% length(nweeks_cyc) + 1L      nweeks <- nweeks_cyc[period]      # Add the extra week to December for Symmetry454 leap years.     # A year is a leap year when (52*year + 146) %% 293 < 52.     m1 <- at * month_size     contains_dec <- which((m1 %% 12L) >= (12L - month_size))     year <- 1970L + m1[contains_dec] %/% 12L     is_leap_year <- ((52 * year + 146L) %% 293L) < 52L      nweeks[contains_dec[is_leap_year]] <- nweeks[contains_dec[is_leap_year]] + 1L      # Scale by the number of weeks in the n-week time granule     nweeks / x@n   }  # The 4-5-4 cycle across a full Symmetry454 year chronon_cardinality(cal_symmetry454$week(1L), cal_symmetry454$month(1L), at = 0:11) #>  [1] 4 5 4 4 5 4 4 5 4 4 5 5  # 1970 is a leap year (53 weeks), so December has 5 weeks instead of 4 chronon_cardinality(cal_symmetry454$week(1L), cal_symmetry454$month(1L), at = 11L) #> [1] 5  # Non-leap year: December has the usual 4 weeks chronon_cardinality(cal_symmetry454$week(1L), cal_symmetry454$month(1L), at = 12:23) # Dec 1971 #>  [1] 4 5 4 4 5 4 4 5 4 4 5 4  # The number of weeks in a multi-month period is the sum of the weeks in each month chronon_cardinality(cal_symmetry454$week(1L), cal_symmetry454$month(2L), at = 0:5) #> [1]  9  8  9  9  8 10 # The number of weeks in a Symmetry454 year (derived from week → month → year) # Note that the leap year in 1970 (at = 0) produces 53 weeks via week → month. chronon_cardinality(cal_symmetry454$week(1L), cal_symmetry454$year(1L), at = 0:4) #> [1] 53 52 52 52 52  # The number of days in a Symmetry454 year (derived from day → week → month → year) chronon_cardinality(cal_symmetry454$day(1L), cal_symmetry454$year(1L), at = 0:4) #> [1] 371 364 364 364 364 # The number of Symmetry454 years in a month is the inverse of months → years (1/12) chronon_cardinality(cal_symmetry454$year(1L), cal_symmetry454$month(1L)) #> [1] 0.08333333  # The number of Symmetry454 months in 2 weeks (requires `at` since weeks → months is irregular) chronon_cardinality(cal_symmetry454$month(1L), cal_symmetry454$week(2L), at = 0:4) #> [1] 0.5 0.4 0.5 0.5 0.4

title: Extending mixtime
text: divmod operation combined division modulus converts time granules defined chronon_divmod() methods. methods required efficiently convert units irregular relationships, days → months. regular relationships (e.g. days → weeks) divmod automatically derived chronon_cardinality() methods alone. chronon_divmod(, , x) converts times x granules time points granules. example, date “1970-02-15” day 45 since epoch (x = 45 = cal_gregorian$day(1L)), converted months (= cal_gregorian$month(1L)) gives 1 month 14 days (div = 1, mod = 14). Methods chronon_divmod() return list : div - quotient (chronons granules) mod - remainder (leftover chronons) Note time values zero-indexed, $div = 0 January 1970, $mod = 0 first day month. result can interpreted $div = 1 February 1970 $mod = 14 14 remaining days (.e. 15th). Symmetry454 calendar irregular cardinality weeks → months (4–5–4 pattern leap weeks), divmod method required. key challenge correctly accounting 293-year leap year pattern. Symmetry454 leap weeks follow structured sub-cycle pattern: 293 years decompose five groups (45+79+45+79+45 years), decomposes primary 17-year sub-cycles (3 leap years) secondary 11-year sub-cycles (2 leap years). majority following code uses structure efficiently count many leap weeks occurred given week number cycle. general strategy used code first remove complexity 293-year leap week cycle regularising week count fixed 52 weeks per year (essentially subtracting cumulative leap weeks cycle). regularised week count easier work 4-5-4 pattern, use find converted month (div) number complete n-month cycles plus number complete n-months within current cycle, regularised week remainder (mod) remaining weeks within current n-month period. final step adjust remainder account leap weeks removed regularisation. complication relates leap week pattern, chronon_divmod() methods complicated generality converting n-unit time granules (e.g. converting 1 week 2 month chronons). generality needed, method can raise error time granule size 1 (e.g. (@n != 1L) stop("...")). inverse relationship must also defined convert months → weeks. converting coarser → finer units, integer values x introduce temporal indeterminacy. convention divmod methods left-align conversion (use first moment month). brevity, inverse divmod method shown can found package’s cal_sym454 source code direct chronon_divmod() method defined pair units, mixtime attempt derive chaining together divmod operations along shortest path intermediate units.
code: chronon_divmod(cal_gregorian$day(1L), cal_gregorian$month(1L), 45L) #> $div #> [1] 1 #>  #> $mod #> [1] 14 S7::method(chronon_divmod, list(cal_symmetry454$week, cal_symmetry454$month)) <-   function(from, to, x) {     # Most of this code works on 1-week granules     week_size <- from@n     x <- x * week_size  # convert n-weeks to 1-weeks      # 1. Account for leap weeks by regularising x to have a fixed 52 weeks per year      # The symmetrical sub-cycles of the 293-year leap week cycle are:     #   17+11+17 + 17+17+11+17+17 + 17+11+17 + 17+17+11+17+17 + 17+11+17     #   = 45 + 79 + 45 + 79 + 45 = 293     # Primary (length 17) sub-cycles have 3 leap years: 00100000100000100     # Secondary (length 11) sub-cycles have 2 leap years: 00100000100     leaps_cycle_17 <- function(w) (w >= 157L) + (w >= 470L) + (w >= 783L)     leaps_cycle_11 <- function(w) (w >= 157L) + (w >= 470L)     leaps_cycle_45 <- function(w) {       ifelse(w < 887L,  leaps_cycle_17(w),       ifelse(w < 1461L, 3L + leaps_cycle_11(w - 887L),                         5L + leaps_cycle_17(w - 1461L)))     }     leaps_cycle_79 <- function(w) {       ifelse(w < 887L,  leaps_cycle_17(w),       ifelse(w < 1774L, 3L  + leaps_cycle_17(w - 887L),       ifelse(w < 2348L, 6L  + leaps_cycle_11(w - 1774L),       ifelse(w < 3235L, 8L  + leaps_cycle_17(w - 2348L),                         11L + leaps_cycle_17(w - 3235L)))))     }     leaps_symmetry454 <- function(x) {       # There are 15288 weeks in a full 293-year cycle (293*52 + 52 leap weeks)       w <- x %% 15288L       x %/% 15288L * 52L +       ifelse(w < 2348L,  leaps_cycle_45(w),       ifelse(w < 6470L,  8L  + leaps_cycle_79(w - 2348L),       ifelse(w < 8818L,  22L + leaps_cycle_45(w - 6470L),       ifelse(w < 12940L, 30L + leaps_cycle_79(w - 8818L),                          44L + leaps_cycle_45(w - 12940L)))))     }      # Offset x to align with the nearest 293-year cycle boundary before the epoch.     # There are 349 leap years between year 1-W1 and the 1970-W1 epoch, and the     # nearest cycle start is (1969*52 + 349) %% (293*52 + 52) = 11009 weeks before epoch.     x_cyc <- x + 11009L + week_size  # right align multi-week granules     n_leaps <- leaps_symmetry454(x_cyc)      # Regularise x to have exactly 52 weeks per year by subtracting leap weeks.     # (37 leap years occur in the cycle before the epoch, so we add 37 back.)     x_reg <- x - n_leaps + 37L      # 2. Use the 4-5-4 pattern to find the month (div) and week remainder (mod)     ## The number of weeks in each n-month period     month_size <- to@n     weeks_len <- circsum(c(4L, 5L, 4L), month_size)      ## The total weeks in a full n-month cycle     weeks_tot <- sum(weeks_len)      ## Find which n-month cycle we're in based on the regularised week count     period_full <- x_reg %/% weeks_tot      ## Find which part within the n-month cycle we're in     weeks_seq  <- cumsum(weeks_len[-length(weeks_len)])     period_part <- rowSums(outer(x_reg %% weeks_tot, weeks_seq, ">="))      # div: total complete n-month cycles + complete n-months within the current cycle     div <- period_full * length(weeks_len) + period_part     # mod: remaining (regularised) weeks within the current n-month period     mod <- x_reg %% weeks_tot - c(0L, weeks_seq)[period_part + 1L]      # 3. Adjust the remainder to account for leap weeks that were removed during regularisation.      # Identify leap weeks re-using cumulative in-cycle leap week counts: leaps_symmetry454()     # If the week added a leap week from the previous, then it itself must be a leap week.     # Applied only to regularised 52/53rd weeks of the year (only these weeks can be leap weeks)     last_weeks  <- which(x_reg %% 52L >= 51L)     leap_weeks  <- last_weeks[n_leaps[last_weeks] - leaps_symmetry454(x_cyc[last_weeks] - week_size) > 0L]     mod[leap_weeks] <- mod[leap_weeks] + 1L  # restore leap week to remainder      # Scale mod back to the original n-unit week size     mod <- mod %/% week_size      # Return the divmod result     list(div = div, mod = mod)   } # Week 19 (0-indexed) of 1970 is the 2nd week (div=1) of May 1970 (mod=4) with(cal_symmetry454, chronon_divmod(week(1L), month(1L), 18L)) #> $div #> [1] 4 #>  #> $mod #> [1] 1  # Week 52 (0-indexed) is the leap week of 1970; it is the 5th week (div=4) of Dec 1970 (mod=11) with(cal_symmetry454, chronon_divmod(week(1L), month(1L), 52L)) #> $div #> [1] 11 #>  #> $mod #> [1] 4 # The 5th fortnight of 1970 is the 3rd fortnight (div=2) of Feb 1970 (mod=1) with(cal_symmetry454, chronon_divmod(week(2L), month(1L), 4L)) #> $div #> [1] 1 #>  #> $mod #> [1] 2 # Divmod for converting days → years is derived from days → weeks → months → years # Gregorian day 839 since unix epoch (1972-04-19) is symmetry454 day 101 (mod=100) of year 1972 (div=2) with(cal_symmetry454, chronon_divmod(day(1L), year(1L), 839L)) #> $div #> [1] 2 #>  #> $mod #> [1] 100

code: # Labels for months of the year, essentially the same as Gregorian months in years. S7::method(cyclical_labels, list(cal_symmetry454$month, cal_symmetry454$year)) <-   function(granule, cycle, i, label = FALSE, abbreviate = FALSE, ...) {     if (label) {       # Index into R's localised month name objects (month.name and month.abb)       if (abbreviate) month.abb[i + 1L] else month.name[i + 1L]     } else {       # Use i + 1L for 1-indexing months (so January is 1, February is 2, ...)       sprintf("%02d", i + 1L)     }   }  # Labels for weeks of the month are simply 1-indexed (e.g. "W1", "W2", ...) S7::method(cyclical_labels, list(cal_symmetry454$week, cal_symmetry454$month)) <-   function(granule, cycle, i, ...) {     as.character(i + 1L)   } # Month of year month_of_year(as.Date("1955-11-12"), calendar = cal_symmetry454) #> <mixtime[1]> #> [1] M11 # Week of month cyclical_time(as.Date("1955-11-12"), chronon = cal_symmetry454$week(1L), cycle = cal_symmetry454$month(1L)) #> <mixtime[1]> #> [1] W2  # Day of week (inherited from cal_isoweek) day_of_week(as.Date("1955-11-12"), calendar = cal_symmetry454) #> <mixtime[1]> #> [1] Sat # Day of year (inherited from cal_time_civil) day_of_year(as.Date("1955-11-12"), calendar = cal_symmetry454) #> <mixtime[1]> #> [1] D363 date(as.Date("1955-11-12"), calendar = cal_symmetry454) #> <mixtime[1]> #> [1] 1955-11-13 yearweek(as.Date("1955-11-12"), calendar = cal_symmetry454) #> <mixtime[1]> #> [1] 1955 W52 yearmonth(as.Date("1955-11-12"), calendar = cal_symmetry454) #> <mixtime[1]> #> [1] M-170

code: # Simply display years as numbers (e.g. "1970", "1971", ...) S7::method(   chronon_format_linear,   list(cal_symmetry454$year, S7::class_any) ) <- function(x, cal) "{lin(year(1L))}"  # Display labelled months as month within year (e.g. "1970 Jan", "1970 Feb", ...) S7::method(   chronon_format_linear,   list(cal_symmetry454$month, S7::class_any) ) <- function(x, cal) "{lin(year(1L))}-{cyc(month(1L), year(1L), label=TRUE, abbreviate=TRUE)}"  # Display weeks as week in month in year (e.g. "1970-01-W1", "1970-01-W2", ...) S7::method(   chronon_format_linear,   list(cal_symmetry454$week, S7::new_S3_class("cal_symmetry454")) ) <- function(x, cal) "{lin(year(1L))}-{cyc(month(1L), year(1L), label=TRUE, abbreviate=TRUE)}-W{cyc(week(1L), month(1L))}"  # Format days as day in week in month in year (e.g. "1970-Jan-W1-Mon", "1970-Jan-W1-Tue", ...) S7::method(   chronon_format_linear,   list(cal_symmetry454$day, S7::new_S3_class("cal_symmetry454")) ) <- function(x, cal) "{lin(year(1L))}-{cyc(month(1L), year(1L), label=TRUE, abbreviate=TRUE)}-W{cyc(week(1L), month(1L))}-{cyc(day(1L), week(1L), label=TRUE, abbreviate=TRUE)}" # Years are formatted as YYYY year(as.Date("1955-11-12"), calendar = cal_symmetry454) #> <mixtime[1]> #> [1] 1955  # Months are formatted as YYYY Mon yearmonth(as.Date("1955-11-12"), calendar = cal_symmetry454) #> <mixtime[1]> #> [1] 1955-Nov  # Weeks are formatted as YYYY-MM-WW yearweek(as.Date("1955-11-12"), calendar = cal_symmetry454) #> <mixtime[1]> #> [1] 1955-Nov-W2  # Days are formatted as YYYY-MM-WW-DD linear_time(as.Date(c("1985-10-26", "1955-11-05", "1955-11-12")), chronon = cal_symmetry454$day(1L)) #> <mixtime[3]> #> [1] 1985-Oct-W4-Sat 1955-Nov-W1-Sat 1955-Nov-W2-Sat

title: Time format strings
text: strftime() uses %-prefixed codes tied Gregorian calendar (e.g. %Y year, %m month), mixtime uses glue-style {...} placeholders refer explicitly time components defined linear cyclical granules. granule cycle arguments accept time granules calendar, example format codes yearmonth() lin(year(1L)) cyc(month(1L), year(1L)). format codes combined time format string glue substitutions: "{lin(year(1L))}-{cyc(month(1L), year(1L))}". linear labels produced linear_labels(), cyclical labels produced cyclical_labels(). formatting mixtime vectors, additional arguments passed lin() cyc() forwarded label helper functions. Common arguments include label = TRUE get named labels (e.g. “January”), abbreviate = TRUE short names (e.g. “Jan” instead “January”). Default format strings chronon (cycle) provided methods chronon_format_linear() chronon_format_cyclical() generics (see vignette("extending-mixtime")). example, default format string time 1-month chronon "{lin(year)}-{cyc(month, year)}": granules used format string calendar-aware, inherit attributes data formatted. includes calendar (e.g. cal_gregorian) granule attributes (e.g. time zone civil time location astronomical time). Explicitly specifying calendar granule attributes format string allows different calendars granule attributes combined format string. example, lunar phase can added date format string including phase granule cal_time_lunar calendar. can also set emoji = TRUE get lunar phase emoji instead numeric index: makes relationship format string calendar structure transparent, means format-string idiom works unchanged across calendars.
code: format(date(Sys.Date()), format = "{lin(year(1L))}-{cyc(month(1L), year(1L))}") #> [1] "2026-06" format(date(Sys.Date()), format = "{lin(year)} {cyc(month, year, label = TRUE, abbreviate = TRUE)}") #> [1] "2026 Jun" format(linear_time(Sys.Date(), chronon = cal_gregorian$month(1L))) #> [1] "2026 Jun" fmt_date  <- "{lin(year)}-{cyc(month, year)}-{cyc(day, month)}" fmt_lunar <- "{with(cal_time_lunar, cyc(phase, month, emoji = TRUE))}" format(date(Sys.Date()), format = paste(fmt_date, fmt_lunar)) #> [1] "2026-06-28 🌔"

what: Comparison with POSIX time format strings
title: Time format strings
text: POSIX time format strings concise require memorising large number %-prefixed codes platform-dependent mostly specific Gregorian calendar. contrast, mixtime format strings verbose intuitive generalisable different calendars time formats. ISO-8601 date (YYYY-MM-DD) represented mixtime format string "{lin(year)}-{cyc(month, year)}-{cyc(day, month)}". verbose equivalent strftime format string ("%Y-%m-%d"), mixtime string descriptive generalisable combinations granules. simplifies construction less common time formats, example year-day format (YYYY-DDD), "{lin(year)}-D{cyc(day, year)}" mixtime "%Y-D%j" strftime (%j non-obvious code “day year”). Format strings non-Gregorian calendars also intuitive, since granules specified reference units calendar systems. example, format string ISO week dates (YYYY-WW-DDD) "{lin(year)}-W{cyc(week, year)}-{cyc(day, week, label = TRUE)}" mixtime. equivalent strftime format string ("%G-W%V-%") requires knowing %G/%V ISO week variants %Y/%W - nuanced distinction easy get wrong. table maps every strftime/strptime conversion specification mixtime equivalent. many possible mixtime format strings don’t direct strftime equivalent.
code: # YYYY-MM-DD format for a Gregorian date format(date(Sys.Date()), format = "{lin(year)}-{cyc(month, year)}-{cyc(day, month)}") #> [1] "2026-06-28" format(Sys.Date(), format = "%Y-%m-%d") #> [1] "2026-06-28" # YYY-DDD format for a Gregorian date format(date(Sys.Date()), format = "{lin(year)}-D{cyc(day, year)}") #> [1] "2026-D179" format(Sys.Date(), format = "%Y-D%j") #> [1] "2026-D179" # YYYY-WW-DD format for a ISO week date format(   date(Sys.Date(), calendar = cal_isoweek),   format = "{lin(year)}-W{cyc(week, year)}-{cyc(day, week, label = TRUE)}" ) #> [1] "2026-W26-Sun" format(Sys.Date(), format = "%G-W%V-%a") #> [1] "2026-W26-Sun"

title: Mixed Temporal Vectors and Operations
text: mixtime provides flexible time classes time series analysis forecasting mixed temporal granularities. designed handling temporal data different frequencies, making ideal : Changing observation frequencies (e.g., historical quarterly records now reported monthly) Analysis involving data different temporal resolutions (e.g., quarterly tourism monthly turnover) Forecasting temporal reconciliation across multiple time scales

title: Mixed Temporal Vectors and Operations
text: 📈 Linear Time - Create linear time vectors linear_time() helpers: year(), yearquarter(), yearmonth(), yearweek(), date(), datetime() 🔄 Cyclical Time - Create cyclical time vectors cyclical_time() helpers: month_of_year(), day_of_year(), day_of_month(), time_of_day(), day_of_week(), week_of_year() ⏳ Time durations - Create time duration vectors duration() helpers: years(), quarters(), months(), weeks(), days(), hours(), minutes(), seconds(), milliseconds() 🕰️ Temporal semantics Discrete continuous time models Timezone support chronons Custom granule sizes (e.g. week(2L) fortnights) Mixed granularity vectors different temporal resolution time (series) 📅 Calendar Systems - Support several calendars: cal_gregorian - Gregorian dates (e.g. 2026-02-17) cal_isoweek - ISO week dates (e.g. 2026-W8-Tue) cal_sym454 - Symmetry454 dates (e.g. 2026-Feb-W4-Tue) calendars coming, including custom censored calendars 🧮 Temporal Operations Rounding: time_floor(), time_round(), time_ceiling() Sequencing: seq() linear cyclical time points

code: library(mixtime) #>  #> Attaching package: 'mixtime' #> The following objects are masked from 'package:base': #>  #>     date, months, quarters demo_time <- as.POSIXct("2026-02-22 18:30:42", tz = "UTC") demo_date <- as.Date("2026-02-22")

title: Mixed Temporal Vectors and Operations
text: Calendars cal_* prefix, contain set time units can accessed <cal>$<unit>. Using time units functions produces time granules.
code: # The Gregorian calendar cal_gregorian #> <cal_gregorian> #> Time units: #>   - year #>   - quarter #>   - month #>   - day #>   - ampm #>   - hour #>   - minute #>   - second #>   - millisecond  # A 1-month time granule cal_gregorian$month(1L) # (1L is integer 1) #> <mixtime::tu_month> #>  @ n : int 1 #>  @ tz: 'mt_naive' chr NA  # A 2-week time granule (fortnights) cal_isoweek$week(2L) #> <mixtime::tu_week> #>  @ n : int 2 #>  @ tz: 'mt_naive' chr NA

title: Mixed Temporal Vectors and Operations
text: linear time vector uses time granules define resolution time points, known chronon (smallest granules time). input time different resolution chronon, automatically converted. Discrete time models (integer-based values) used default, however continuous time models (double-based values) can used discrete = FALSE allow fractional chronons. Linear time helper functions available quickly create common time points. mixtime package allows time different granulities combined single vector.
code: linear_time(demo_date, chronon = cal_gregorian$month(1L)) #> <mixtime[1]> #> [1] 2026 Feb # February 22nd is 75% through the month (in non-leap years) linear_time(demo_date, chronon = cal_gregorian$month(1L), discrete = FALSE) #> <mixtime[1]> #> [1] 2026 Feb 75.0% # Create time vectors at different granularities yearquarter(demo_date) + 0:7 #> <mixtime[8]> #> [1] 2026 Q1 2026 Q2 2026 Q3 2026 Q4 2027 Q1 2027 Q2 2027 Q3 2027 Q4 yearmonth(demo_date) + 0:11 #> <mixtime[12]> #>  [1] 2026 Feb 2026 Mar 2026 Apr 2026 May 2026 Jun 2026 Jul 2026 Aug 2026 Sep 2026 Oct 2026 Nov #> [11] 2026 Dec 2027 Jan yearweek(demo_date) + 0:10 #> <mixtime[11]> #>  [1] 2026 W08 2026 W09 2026 W10 2026 W11 2026 W12 2026 W13 2026 W14 2026 W15 2026 W16 2026 W17 #> [11] 2026 W18 date(demo_date) + 0:6 #> <mixtime[7]> #> [1] 2026-02-22 2026-02-23 2026-02-24 2026-02-25 2026-02-26 2026-02-27 2026-02-28 c(   year(demo_date), yearquarter(demo_date),    yearmonth(demo_date), yearweek(demo_date) ) #> <mixtime[4]> #> [1] 2026     2026 Q1  2026 Feb 2026 W08

title: Mixed Temporal Vectors and Operations
text: cyclical time vector defined two time granules: chronon (smaller granule time) cycle (larger granule chronon loops ). several cyclical time helper functions convenience.
code: # The `calendar` argument provides a masking scope to `chronon` and `cycle` cyclical_time(demo_date, chronon = day(1L), cycle = week(1L), calendar = cal_isoweek) #> <mixtime[1]> #> [1] Sun # Extract cyclical components month_of_year(demo_date) #> <mixtime[1]> #> [1] Feb week_of_year(demo_date) #> <mixtime[1]> #> [1] W08 day_of_week(demo_date) #> <mixtime[1]> #> [1] Sun  # Continuous cyclical time shows progress through chronons day_of_week(demo_time, discrete = FALSE) #> <mixtime[1]> #> [1] Sun 77.1%

title: Mixed Temporal Vectors and Operations
text: linear cyclical time vectors support timezones via tz argument.
code: demo_time #> [1] "2026-02-22 18:30:42 UTC" # Same day (Sunday) in LA date(demo_time, tz = "America/Los_Angeles") #> <mixtime[1]> #> [1] 2026-02-22 PST date(demo_time, tz = "America/Los_Angeles", discrete = FALSE) #> <mixtime[1]> #> [1] 2026-02-22 43.8% PST day_of_week(demo_time, tz = "America/Los_Angeles") #> <mixtime[1]> #> [1] Sun PST  # Next day (Monday) in Melbourne (23% through the 23rd) date(demo_time, tz = "Australia/Melbourne") #> <mixtime[1]> #> [1] 2026-02-23 AEDT date(demo_time, tz = "Australia/Melbourne", discrete = FALSE) #> <mixtime[1]> #> [1] 2026-02-23 23.0% AEDT day_of_week(demo_time, tz = "Australia/Melbourne") #> <mixtime[1]> #> [1] Mon AEDT

title: Mixed Temporal Vectors and Operations
text: Linear time points can adjusted floor, ceiling, rounded specified time granule.
code: # Round dates to different granularities time_floor(demo_date, cal_gregorian$month(1L)) #> [1] "2026-02-01" time_round(demo_date, cal_isoweek$week(1L)) #> [1] "2026-02-23" time_ceiling(demo_date, cal_gregorian$month(1L)) #> [1] "2026-03-01"

title: Mixed Temporal Vectors and Operations
text: seq() function creates sequences time points iterating given time granule.
code: # Integer increments (advances by chronon's natural granule) seq(yearmonth(demo_date), by = 1L, length.out = 10) #> <mixtime[10]> #>  [1] 2026 Feb 2026 Mar 2026 Apr 2026 May 2026 Jun 2026 Jul 2026 Aug 2026 Sep 2026 Oct 2026 Nov  # Time can be sequenced by different granules than the chronon seq(date(demo_date), by = cal_gregorian$month(1L), length.out = 8) #> <mixtime[8]> #> [1] 2026-02-22 2026-03-22 2026-04-22 2026-05-22 2026-06-22 2026-07-22 2026-08-22 2026-09-22

title: Symmetry454 time unit classes — calendar_sym454
text: Symmetry454 calendar (Sym454) perennial solar calendar proposed Dr. Irv Bromberg. preserves traditional 12-month structure 7-day week, months arranged symmetrical 4-5-4 week pattern per quarter. Every month starts Monday whole number weeks, meaning month ever contains partial week. following time units available Symmetry454 calendar (cal_sym454$). year(): Year unit month(): Month unit week(): Week unit day(): Day unit hour(): Hour unit minute(): Minute unit second(): Second unit millisecond(): Millisecond unit

title: Symmetry454 time unit classes — calendar_sym454
text: Rather intercalary days, Symmetry454 uses leap week appended December every 5 6 years, making December 5-week month leap years. year leap year (52 * year + 146) %% 293 < 52. yields mean year 365 + 71/293 days (approx 365 days, 5 hours, 48 minutes, 56.5 seconds), intentionally slightly shorter mean northward equinoctial year.

title: Cardinality between time granules — chronon_cardinality
code: # There are 12 months in a year with(cal_gregorian, chronon_cardinality(month(1L), year(1L))) #> [1] 12  # There are 7 days in a week with(cal_isoweek, chronon_cardinality(day(1L), week(1L))) #> [1] 7  # There are 3600 seconds in an hour with(cal_gregorian, chronon_cardinality(second(1L), hour(1L))) #> [1] 3600  # There are 18 "2 months" in 3 years with(cal_gregorian, chronon_cardinality(month(2L), year(3L))) #> [1] 18  # There are 365 days in 2025 (a common year) chronon_cardinality(   cal_gregorian$day(1L), cal_gregorian$year(1L),   at = year(as.Date("2025-01-01")) ) #> [1] 365  # There are 366 days in 2024 (a leap year) chronon_cardinality(   cal_gregorian$day(1L), cal_gregorian$year(1L),    at = mixtime::year(as.Date("2024-01-01")) ) #> [1] 366  # There are 29 days in February 2024 (a leap year) chronon_cardinality(   cal_gregorian$day(1L), cal_gregorian$month(1L),    at = yearmonth(as.Date("2024-02-01")) ) #> [1] 29

title: Cyclical time points — cyclical_time
text: data Input data convert cyclical time. Can : Numeric values (interpreted chronons, 1-indexed) Character strings (parsed dates/times) Date POSIXct objects time objects chronon time granule representing chronon (finest indivisible time granule), evaluated context calendar. Use unquoted expressions like day(1L) month(1L). Chronons specific calendar can also used (e.g. cal_isoweek$day(1L)). cycle time granule representing cycle (coarser time granule defines period), evaluated context calendar. Use unquoted expressions like week(1L) year(1L). discrete Logical. TRUE (default), returns integer positions within cycle (discrete time model). what: mixtime: Mixed Temporal Vectors and Operations — mixtime-package
title: mixtime: Mixed Temporal Vectors and Operations — mixtime-package
text: Flexible time classes time series analysis forecasting mixed temporal granularities. Supports linear cyclical time representations discrete continuous forms, timezone support, across multiple calendar systems including Gregorian ISO week date calendars. Time points stored numerically relative chronon; atomic time granule defined time units calendar. Calendrical arithmetic enables conversion time granules (e.g. days months) calendar systems. Multi-unit arithmetic allows temporal analysis granules common calendars (e.g. fortnights 2-week units). Time vectors different granularities (e.g. monthly quarterly) can combined single vector, making 'mixtime' ideal data changes observation frequency time requires temporal reconciliation across scales. package extensible, allowing users define custom calendars build upon civil astronomical time systems.

title: mixtime: Mixed Temporal Vectors and Operations — mixtime-package
text: Maintainer: Mitchell O'Hara-Wild mail@mitchelloharawild.com (ORCID) Authors: Mitchell O'Hara-Wild mail@mitchelloharawild.com (ORCID)

title: Create a mixtime vector — mixtime
text: data vector time values. can character vector (e.g. "2024-01-01"), numeric vector (e.g. seconds since epoch), time class (e.g. Date, POSIXct, yearmonth, etc.). chronon time granule object representing smallest indivisible time granule (chronon) mixtime. used interpret numeric values data define time resolution mixtime. provided, inferred data. cycle optional time granule object representing cycle cyclical time. used define repeating cycle cyclical time representations (e.g. day--week, month--year). provided, mixtime treated linear time. discrete logical indicating whether time values treated discrete (integer) continuous (fractional). affects numeric values interpreted time arithmetic performed. default TRUE (discrete).

title: Create a mixtime vector — mixtime
text: mixtime object representing time values data according specified chronon cycle.

title: Create a mixtime vector — mixtime
code: # Create a mixtime for today mixtime(Sys.Date()) #> <mixtime[1]> #> [1] 2026-06-28  # Create a mixtime for the current date and time mixtime(Sys.time()) #> <mixtime[1]> #> [1] 2026-06-28 12:40:02  # Convert time from tsibble classes to mixtime mixtime(tsibble::yearmonth("2024 Jan")) #> <mixtime[1]> #> [1] 2024 Jan  # Create a mixtime for the time of day (cyclical time) mixtime(Sys.time(), cycle = cal_gregorian$day(1L)) #> <mixtime[1]> #> [1] 12:40:03  # Specify a timezone for the chronon mixtime(Sys.time(), chronon = cal_gregorian$second(1L, tz = Sys.timezone())) #> <mixtime[1]> #> [1] 2026-06-28 12:40:03 mixtime(Sys.time(), chronon = cal_gregorian$second(1L, tz = "Pacific/Honolulu")) #> <mixtime[1]> #> [1] 2026-06-28 02:40:03 HST mixtime(Sys.time(), chronon = cal_gregorian$second(1L, tz = "Australia/Melbourne")) #> <mixtime[1]> #> [1] 2026-06-28 22:40:03 AEST  # Dates (and all granularities) can have timezones mixtime(Sys.time(), chronon = cal_gregorian$day(1L, tz = Sys.timezone())) #> <mixtime[1]> #> [1] 2026-06-28 mixtime(Sys.time(), chronon = cal_gregorian$day(1L, tz = "Pacific/Honolulu")) #> <mixtime[1]> #> [1] 2026-06-28 HST mixtime(Sys.time(), chronon = cal_gregorian$day(1L, tz = "Australia/Melbourne")) #> <mixtime[1]> #> [1] 2026-06-28 AEST  # Continuous time tracks progress within the chronon mixtime(Sys.time(), chronon = cal_gregorian$day(1L, tz = Sys.timezone()), discrete = FALSE) #> <mixtime[1]> #> [1] 2026-06-28 52.8%  # Mixtime can combine different granularities and timezones in a vector now <- Sys.time() c(   # Datetime (second chronon) in UTC   mixtime(now),   # Date (minute chronon) in local timezone   mixtime(now, chronon = cal_gregorian$minute(1L, tz = Sys.timezone())),   # Month (month chronon) in UTC   mixtime(now, chronon = cal_gregorian$month(1L)) ) #> <mixtime[3]> #> [1] 2026-06-28 12:40:03 2026-06-28 12:40    2026 Jun

title: Base S7 class for creating new time units — mt_unit
text: Time units building blocks calendars mixtime. unit represents specific temporal component (e.g., day, month, year) can combined using new_calendar() create calendar system. creating custom calendars, define time unit classes inherit either mt_unit (standard units) mt_tz_unit (timezone-aware units), pass named arguments new_calendar(). calendar use names create constructor functions accessible via $ notation (e.g., calendar$day(1L)).

title: Base S7 class for creating new time units — mt_unit
text: Time units enable calendar arithmetic two key generic methods implemented custom time units: chronon_cardinality(x, y, ) - Returns number x granule fit within one y granule can fixed value (e.g., 7 days per week) variable based (e.g., 28-31 days per month). chronon_divmod(x, , ) - Converts time point x granules granules , returning list div (quotient) mod. enables conversions granules variable cardinality (e.g., date 2020-03-23 month 2020-03). conversions based chronons since epoch (1970-01-01), UTC time zone. methods work together enable mixtime perform calendar-aware arithmetic, understanding months variable lengths handling timezone-aware conversions.

title: Generate sequences of mixtime values — seq.mixtime::mixtime
text: ... Additional arguments passed underlying sequence method. Starting value sequence. End value sequence (provided). Increment sequence. Can : numeric number time chronons character string specifying interval (e.g., "1 day", "2 weeks", "1 month", "1 year") time granule object created time unit functions (e.g., cal_gregorian$year(1L), cal_gregorian$month(1L), cal_gregorian$day(1L)) time duration() object (e.g., years(1L), months(1L), days(1L)) length.Desired length sequence (alternative ). along.Take length argument (alternative length.). on_invalid handle time points overflow cycle using argument different time granule sequence chronon. Options : "nearest" (default): Adjust overflowing time points nearest valid time point within cycle "overflow": Allow time points overflow next cycle relevant starting time point offset exist cycles. example, starting day 31 = "1 month" overflow months fewer 31 days (e.g., February). "nearest", adjusted last day month (e.g., Feb 28/29). "overflow", extra days carry next month. explicitly specified overflow occurs, warning issued default "nearest" behavior applied.

title: Generate sequences of mixtime values — seq.mixtime::mixtime
code: # Linear time sequences with integer by seq(yearmonth("2020-01-01"), yearmonth("2020-12-01")) #> <mixtime[12]> #>  [1] 2020 Jan 2020 Feb 2020 Mar 2020 Apr 2020 May 2020 Jun 2020 Jul 2020 Aug #>  [9] 2020 Sep 2020 Oct 2020 Nov 2020 Dec seq(yearquarter("2020-01-01"), length.out = 5, by = 3) #> <mixtime[5]> #> [1] 2020 Q1 2020 Q4 2021 Q3 2022 Q2 2023 Q1  # Linear time sequences with string intervals seq(date("2020-01-01"), date("2020-12-31"), by = "1 month") #> <mixtime[12]> #>  [1] 2020-01-01 2020-02-01 2020-03-01 2020-04-01 2020-05-01 2020-06-01 #>  [7] 2020-07-01 2020-08-01 2020-09-01 2020-10-01 2020-11-01 2020-12-01 seq(yearmonth("2020-01-01"), yearmonth("2025-01-01"), by = "1 year") #> <mixtime[6]> #> [1] 2020 Jan 2021 Jan 2022 Jan 2023 Jan 2024 Jan 2025 Jan seq(date("2020-01-01"), length.out = 10, by = "2 weeks") #> <mixtime[10]> #>  [1] 2020-01-01 2020-01-15 2020-01-29 2020-02-12 2020-02-26 2020-03-11 #>  [7] 2020-03-25 2020-04-08 2020-04-22 2020-05-06  # Linear time sequences incrementing by time granules seq(yearmonth("2020-01-01"), yearmonth("2020-12-01"), by = cal_gregorian$month(2L)) #> <mixtime[6]> #> [1] 2020 Jan 2020 Mar 2020 May 2020 Jul 2020 Sep 2020 Nov seq(date("2020-01-01"), length.out = 5, by = cal_gregorian$year(1L)) #> <mixtime[5]> #> [1] 2020-01-01 2021-01-01 2022-01-01 2023-01-01 2024-01-01 seq(date("2020-01-01"), date("2020-01-31"), by = cal_gregorian$day(7L)) #> <mixtime[5]> #> [1] 2020-01-01 2020-01-08 2020-01-15 2020-01-22 2020-01-29  # Handling invalid dates with on_invalid seq(date("2020-01-31"), length.out = 3, by = "1 month")  # warns, uses nearest #> Warning: The cycle offset (31 days) has produced time points that overflow the month #> cycle. #> ! Using the nearest valid time points in the cycle, `on_invalid = "nearest"` #>   (the default). #> ℹ Specify `on_invalid` explicitly to suppress this warning. #> <mixtime[3]> #> [1] 2020-01-31 2020-02-29 2020-03-31 seq(date("2020-01-31"), length.out = 3, by = "1 month", on_invalid = "nearest") #> <mixtime[3]> #> [1] 2020-01-31 2020-02-29 2020-03-31 seq(date("2020-01-31"), length.out = 3, by = "1 month", on_invalid = "overflow") #> <mixtime[3]> #> [1] 2020-01-31 2020-03-02 2020-03-31  # Cyclical time sequences seq(month_of_year(0L), month_of_year(11L)) #> <mixtime[12]> #>  [1] Jan Feb Mar Apr May Jun Jul Aug Sep Oct Nov Dec seq(day_of_week(0L), day_of_week(6L), by = 1) #> <mixtime[7]> #> [1] Thu Fri Sat Sun Mon Tue Wed

title: mixtime 0.2.0
text: Added support multiplication division time durations. Added round()/floor()/ceiling() methods rounding time chronons. (note: use time_round(), time_floor(), time_ceiling() rounding specified time granule). Added casting time durations character vectors. Added explicit conversion naive time using tz = NA.

title: mixtime 0.2.0
text: time_unit_full() now uses cli-style pluralisation templates (e.g., "year{?/s}", "centur{?y/ies}"), enabling accurate plural forms beyond simple s suffix. new time_unit_plural(x, n) helper resolves template given quantity. Continuous time model durations now always show least one decimal place. Added formatting support NaN, Inf, -Inf values time types. Added time zone support arithmetic. Time durations now use formatting system format strings linear cyclical time. Improved str() output compact time type.

title: mixtime 0.2.0
text: Fixed incorrect usage time_chronon() causing arithmetic fail. Fixed incompatibilities vctrs sorting, ptype2, casting methods. Fixed formatting cyclical time mixed-calendar chronon cycle (#62). Fixed divmod Gregorian day -> month day -> year producing incorrect divisors near year boundary continuous time dates. Fixed c() allowing non-time vectors mixtime vectors.

title: mixtime 0.1.0
text: linear_time() creates linear time vectors user-specified chronon (smallest indivisible time granule), supporting discrete (integer) continuous (fractional) time models. Convenience functions common temporal granularities: year(), yearquarter(), yearmonth(), yearweek(), date(), datetime().

title: mixtime 0.1.0
text: cyclical_time() creates cyclical time vectors representing positions within repeating cycle (e.g. day--week, month--year). Convenience functions common cyclical temporal granularities: month_of_year(), day_of_year(), day_of_month(), day_of_week(), week_of_year(), time_of_day().

title: mixtime 0.1.0
text: cal_gregorian: standard Gregorian calendar year, quarter, month, day, hour, minute, second, millisecond units. cal_isoweek: ISO 8601 week-date calendar ISO year, week, day units. Weeks always start Monday; first week year week containing first Thursday. cal_sym454: Symmetry454 perennial solar calendar symmetrical 4–5–4 week pattern per quarter leap-week rule.
